title: Character Backstory Builder
summary: Prompts for tying your character into 1242 CE Athens specifically.
body_markdown:
# Character Backstory Builder

Not every backstory needs to be fully formed at session zero — leave room to discover things in play. A few prompts to get started:

- **Why are you in Athens in 1242 CE?** A local resident, someone sent by your sire, a refugee from elsewhere in the Duchy — pick something that gives you a reason to stay entangled rather than skip town.
- **What's your relationship to the [Triumvirate](/wiki/the-triumvirate)?** Have you met [Dionysius](/wiki/dionysius), [Democritus](/wiki/democritus), or [Olympias](/wiki/olympias)? Do you owe one of them anything?
- **Where do you sit relative to Elysium?** Domain-holder, hopeful climber, or someone who's never set foot in [Cecropia](/wiki/acropolis-cecropia)?
- **What's your Touchstone, and why them specifically** (see [Character Creation Basics](/wiki/character-creation-basics))?
- **Pick a Chronicle Tenet lens** — Athenian, Conspiracy, or write your own — even loosely, to know what your character would never do.

Don't forget to look at [Athens](/wiki/athens), [Clans & Bloodlines of Greece](/wiki/clans-and-bloodlines-of-greece), and the rest of the wiki's Locations for ideas if you're stuck — a lot of backstory hooks fall out of picking a city of origin or a clan's local politics.

title: Character Creation Basics
summary: Age category, Generation, Blood Potency, and XP for building a character in 1242 CE Athens.
body_markdown:
# Character Creation Basics

Characters are built with standard **V5** rules, adjusted for Athens in 1242 CE. Storytellers should feel free to adjust any of this.

## Age, Embrace Year, Generation & Blood Potency

Characters in this era are closer to Caine in time, so starting Generation and Blood Potency run higher than a modern-nights V5 game. Age category is tied to how recently your character was Embraced, relative to the chronicle's start year of 1242 CE:

| Age | Embraced Within | Generation | Blood Potency | XP to Spend |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Childer | Last 15 years (1227–1242 CE) | 12th–13th | 1 | 0 |
| Neonate | Last 80 years (1162–1242 CE) | 10th–11th | 2 | 15 |
| Ancilla | Last 250 years (992–1242 CE) | 8th–9th | 3 | 35 |

XP budgets match the standard V5 corebook Ancilla-mode defaults — this chronicle isn't using the Dark Ages sourcebook's own Sea of Time XP table, and isn't using eras or In Memoriam-style time jumps. One coterie, one starting point: 1242 CE.

## Roads, not just Humanity

Every character still has a Humanity rating, but many Cainites of this era walk a **Road** instead — an alternate morality track with its own texture and social fabric. Roads are represented as Loresheets (see [Loresheets](/wiki/loresheets)) and are available *in addition* to your one starting Loresheet.

## Skill Swaps

A few standard V5 skills don't fit 1242 CE cleanly:

- **Science → Warfare.** Represents large-scale martial knowledge instead.
- **Drive** stays, but represents carriages, ships, and horses.
- **Firearms → Archery.**

## Chronicle Tenets

Before diving into character creation, consider setting Chronicle Tenets with your coterie — lines your characters could cross as the Stories unfold. A few starting options (mix, match, or write your own):

**Athenian**
- Never harm Classical Greek artwork or culture.
- Never damage or endanger the Athenian people as a whole.
- Never let outside politics supersede local vampiric concerns.

**Conspiracy**
- Never betray the trust of the coterie.
- Never put clan or sect above the coterie.
- Acquire status and power at any cost.

## Touchstones

Given how long these characters may exist across the Chronicle, expect Touchstones to be replaced periodically, often by someone similar. You don't need to track the specific mortal's name so much as *the kind of person* your character keeps choosing — when one inevitably dies, you gain a new one without necessarily needing to play out the loss each time.

See also: [Player Options](/wiki/player-options) for Clan, Predator Type, and Coterie Type choices, and [Character Backstory Builder](/wiki/character-backstory-builder) for backstory prompts.

title: Clan Options
summary: Playable clans in 1242 CE Greece and their dark-ages Disciplines.
body_markdown:
# Clan Options

All clans present in Greece in 1242 CE are playable, subject to Storyteller approval — several bloodlines below are unique to this era and region. Disciplines and Banes shown are dark-ages versions and may differ from modern V5 corebook entries.

For each clan's in-fiction politics and presence in Athens, see [Clans & Bloodlines of Greece](/wiki/clans-and-bloodlines-of-greece).

| Clan | Disciplines |
|---|---|
| Banu Haqim (Sorcerer caste) | Auspex, Blood Sorcery, Celerity |
| Banu Haqim (Vizier caste) | Auspex, Blood Sorcery, Presence |
| Brujah | Celerity, Potence, Presence |
| Cappadocian | Auspex, Fortitude, Oblivion |
| Gangrel ("Greek Gangrel") | Obfuscate in place of Fortitude — city-adapted |
| Lasombra | standard |
| Malkavian | standard |
| Nosferatu ("Larvae" bloodline) | tunneling / stone-eating trait, see Loresheet |
| Ravnos | standard |
| Toreador | standard |
| Setite Ministry | standard |
| Tremere | rare in Greece — one known chantry in Thessaloniki |
| Tzimisce | standard |
| Ventrue | standard |

*Full Bane text, the Banu Haqim caste split, and Greek-specific bloodlines (Greek Gangrel, Nosferatu Larvae, Giovani, Lamiae) are detailed in the sourcebook's Appendix A — ask your Storyteller for the page reference for your choice.*

title: Coterie Types
summary: Dark Ages coterie archetypes and their starting Backgrounds, from Book One Appendix A.
body_markdown:
# Coterie Types

The Coterie types found in the *Vampire* corebook work fine for Dark Ages games — nothing wrong with them at all. Some, like the Vehme (protectors of the Masquerade), may not exist yet in this era. Others, like the Cerberus type (protecting a specific site), are probably more common now than in modern nights. Some of the following are altered versions of the modern types — the Sword type, for instance, is quite similar to the Marechal coterie, a prince's enforcement arm.

Coterie types are an important tool for giving a Player group a clear starting goal. Over the centuries that goal may change, and Players may wish to adapt one type into another, or replace the group's Coterie Type completely — times change, and so do Cainites.

## Autarky

The coterie members are united in their shared loathing for Cainite politics. They've set up a place to self-govern without outside rulers or rules, most likely with a beef against the nearest prince or authority figure. They reject the concepts of class and caste among Cainites, associating together despite their motley composition — and may have to change in the coming centuries when the Camarilla and Sabbat force many to choose sides.

- Domain: Chasse 3, Portillon 2
- Enemies 2
- Possible extras: Adversary (the local prince)

## Cacophony

Some love to spend their time at Elysium — dressing up, gossiping, and engaging in short-term power plays. They team up against outsiders and those currently on the outs politically, judging those who deviate from the status quo. They are the hated Harpies: powerful Cainites with untold power to dress down their foes, most likely comprised of the wealthy and fashionable.

- Domain: Chasse 1, Lien 2
- Status 3
- Enemies 3
- Possible extras: Influence (in the mortal court, if your Elysium takes place at one)

## Caravan

Some coteries are constantly on the move, travelling from town to town, city to city. Though travel in the Dark Ages is dangerous — particularly for vampires — the coterie finds a cover or focus for their travelling band, such as a merchant caravan, pilgrimage, or company of actors.

- Domain: Chasse (the caravan's mobile holdings) 1, Portillon 2
- Retainers 2
- Possible extras: Contacts, Influence

## Council

Local Cainite leaders naturally band together for mutual protection and political clout. Though they may not yet hold high stations, they still garner some status and respect through their collective dedication to upholding the Traditions of Caine — and are often asked much by their fellow vampires in return for heightened standing.

- Domain: Chasse 2, Lien 1
- Status 3
- Enemies 1
- Possible extras: Adversary (a proto-Anarch leader rebelling against them)

## Crusade

The group has come together to perform a religious duty — travelling to Outremer to conduct a Cainite version of a crusade, or protecting a church or other religious site. All most likely share a faith and take orders from a more powerful vampire within it. The Catholic church is an obvious basis, but Muslim vampires from the Ashirra sect, or a dwindling pagan faith, are equally possible.

- Domain: Chasse 1, Portillon 1
- Mawla 3
- Status 1
- Enemies 1
- Possible extras: Allies or Retainers within the church

## Cult

Harvesting blood from those who worship you as a god is technically forbidden under the Traditions of Caine, but it's been common practice for thousands of years. The coterie has set themselves up as leaders of such a blood cult, enjoying easily acquired Vitae and an army of worshippers. Keeping the secret is as important as running the religion — codewords, blood oaths, and secret signs help protect the coterie from zealous hunters.

- Domain: Chasse 2, Portillon 1
- Herd 2
- Retainers 1
- Despised (by the Church)
- Dark Secret
- Possible extras: Mask

## Guild

The coterie has come together to engage in commerce, part of the growing merchant class. Money is power in the mortal and Cainite worlds alike, and the wealthy make useful pawns. Based around a specific business enterprise or a guild of artisans or merchants — perhaps, centuries from now, they'll control the corporations that seem to run the world.

- Domain: Chasse 1, Portillon 1
- Resources 3
- Enemies 2 (competitors)
- Possible extras: Retainers (workers)

## Household

The coterie has control over a keep or castle, ruling as a noble lord and the staff who support her. At least one PC should have the Noble Predator type; others could be advisors, masters-at-arms, seneschals, or other members of the entourage. Together they rule over the surrounding land, true princes of the night.

- Domain: Chasse 3, Portillon 2
- Retainers 2
- Adversary 2 (a rival lord)
- Possible extras: Haven, Resources (taxes), Haven Flaws such as Haunted

## Inconnu

The coterie is devoted to the service of a powerful methuselah — a concept that fits particularly well with the War of Princes, the era's conflict between several European ancients for political control. One or more PCs may take the Sycophant Predator Type. This term is currently used generally to describe the unknown pawns of the ancients; in time these characters may even be approached to join the secret Sect of the same name.

- Domain: Chasse 2, Lien 1, Portillon 1
- Mawla 5
- Adversary 5 (an enemy of their master)
- Possible extras: Resources (provided by the methuselah), Enemies

## Sabbat

An uprising of disaffected rebels begins, led by a secret faction known as the [Prometheans](/wiki/the-prometheans). This will eventually lead to the Anarch Revolt and the formation of the Sabbat sect. For now, individual groups of Anarchs are known as "sabbats," conducting themselves in some places as marauding witches in the night. If the coterie works with the Prometheans, they may gain criminal contacts and access to smugglers of stolen and illicit goods.

- Domain: Chasse 2, Lien 1, Portillon 3
- Infamy 3
- Possible extras: Allies or Contacts associated with the nascent Anarch movement

## Shepherds

The coterie has formed around the protection and cultivation of a particular mortal group — a church congregation, a village, an industry, a criminal enterprise, a tavern, or a noble family. The mortals serve as an obvious herd, and a source of ghouls and childer for the future.

- Domain: Chasse 1, Lien 2
- Herd 2
- Enemies 2 (some mortal threat to the community)
- Possible extras: Allies and Retainers from among the mortals there

## Sicarii

The coterie is a secret order of assassins, hired out by Cainites or even mortals to change the destiny of kingdoms. They train together, hide their occupation together, and work together on tough missions. Not officially condoned by the powers-that-be, and not something one brags about — assassins have a very bad reputation, particularly among the Banu Haqim, rumored to be diablerists.

- Domain: Chasse 1, Portillon 3
- Resources 3
- Despised
- Possible extras: Retainers (underling assassins)

## Sword

The coterie works as the enforcers of a prince or other powerful Cainite authority — a sheriff and deputies, or a collection of vampiric brigands who come when beckoned. Should have members who are martially capable, though an investigator or leader could be useful too.

- Domain: Chasse 2, Portillon 2
- Status 3
- Adversary 3 (a local villain)
- Possible extras: Mawla (the prince)

title: Loresheets
summary: Available loresheets by sourcebook. Full tier text lives in the character creator and sourcebooks.
body_markdown:
# Loresheets

This is a name-and-source index of loresheets available in the character creator — not every entry is necessarily era-appropriate for 1242 CE Athens, so check with your Storyteller, especially for anything tied to a specific modern-nights person, place, or event. Reflavoring is always on the table.

Not listed here: **In Memoriam** loresheets — this chronicle isn't using eras or In Memoriam-style time jumps (see [Character Creation Basics](/wiki/character-creation-basics)).

Remember: a Loresheet can also represent your character's **Road**, not just a piece of setting lore — see Character Creation Basics for how Roads work alongside your one starting Loresheet.

title: Player Options
summary: The three big mechanical choices for building a character: Clan, Predator Type, Coterie Type.
body_markdown:
# Player Options

The three big mechanical choices for your character, dark-ages-flavored:

- **[Clan Options](/wiki/clan-options)** — your clan (and possibly bloodline), and which Disciplines it grants in this era.
- **[Predator Types](/wiki/predator-types)** — how you feed.
- **[Coterie Types](/wiki/coterie-types)** — what holds your group together.

For each clan's in-fiction politics and presence in Athens, see [Clans & Bloodlines of Greece](/wiki/clans-and-bloodlines-of-greece).

title: Predator Types
summary: Dark Ages feeding archetypes for character creation, from Book One Appendix A.
body_markdown:
# Predator Types

The following Predator Types are designed for the Dark Ages setting of this chronicle, though nothing stops a character from using the standard types from the *Vampire* corebook instead. Storytellers may disallow any of these, or require PCs to choose from this list — Storyteller's call.

## Camp Follower

*Hunting Rolls: Manipulation + Leadership or Wits + Warfare*

Armies have always had non-soldiers following in their wake, providing aid and comfort to warriors on their long journeys to and from battle. You have become part of such an entourage, feeding upon soldiers as they lie in their tents at night or hunting the battlefields for the dead and dying who still hold Vitae within their veins. When your coterie is away from the battle lines, you hunt the night watch of the city guard and the military barracks you know so well.

Works particularly well for former soldiers, mercenaries, prostitutes, and barbers — even noble types who command troops.

- Add a specialty: Leadership (soldiers) or Warfare (aftermath)
- Gain one dot of Obfuscate or Presence
- Gain either the Iron Gullet Merit or three dots in the Herd Background (soldiers)
- Gain either the Organovore Flaw or both the No Haven and Destitute Flaws

## Cirurgien

*Hunting Rolls: Intelligence + Medicine*

Medicine is not yet a science, but some do make health their specialty. The most learned scholars of the era believe in the humors of the blood, which align with the Resonances known to Cainites. Through blood lettings and leeches, you ostensibly work to balance the forces within the body — in truth, you gorge yourself on the blood you acquire through your ministrations. You may be associated with the Hospitaller Order, a local barber, or the personal cirurgien (surgeon) to a noble lord.

- Add a specialty: Medicine (blood lettings) or Persuasion (nobles)
- Gain one dot of Animalism or Blood Sorcery (Witches of Echidna, Banu Haqim, Tremere, or their bloodlines only)
- Gain either one dot of Humanity or both a dot of Medicine and a dot of Melee
- Gain the Baby Teeth Thin-Blood Flaw, even if you are not thin-blooded

## Confessor

*Hunting Rolls: Manipulation + Persuasion or Intelligence + Academics*

You are either an actual member of the clergy or you know how to pretend to be. You've learned to twist the trust mortals place in you for moral guidance into opportunities to get them alone and take their blood, by force or persuasion. Because you operate around churches, you run the risk of running into Inquisitors — but you're good at what you do.

- Add a specialty: Persuasion (confession) or Academics (Theology)
- Gain one dot of Dominate or Oblivion (Cappadocians, Lasombra, or their bloodlines only)
- Gain Linguistics (Latin) and two dots of the Influence Background
- Gain either two points in Mythic Flaws or the Infamy Flaw at two dots, representing Faithful hunters on your tail

## Courtier

*Hunting Rolls: Charisma + Subterfuge*

You have the status and skill to blend in among those at court, feeding on the wealthy and powerful. You hunt nobles, their entourages, and perhaps even a royal once in a while. Particularly common among Harpies, who spend as much time at mortal court as they do vampiric Elysium. Likely a Ventrue or Toreador, though exceptions exist.

- Add a specialty: Politics (court) or Subterfuge (seduction)
- Gain one dot of Auspex or Presence
- Gain three dots of the Influence Background
- Gain two dots of the Infamy Flaw, representing your political enemies within the mortal court

## Devil

*Hunting Rolls: Manipulation + Insight*

You make devil's bargains with mortals in return for their blood, providing services to those who accept your bargain. In some cases all you need to do is ghoul the mortal to give them what they desire. Many mortals see you as the devil himself or one of his servants, yet you are far more humane in your feeding than those who simply take blood by force.

- Add a specialty: Insight (mortals in need) or Persuasion (devil's bargains)
- Gain one dot of Auspex or Dominate
- Gain one dot of Humanity or three dots in the Retainers Background
- Gain the Flaw: Prey Exclusion (non-consenting)
- Gain three Stains whenever you break a promise to a mortal

## Entertainer

*Hunting Rolls: Charisma + Performance*

You work your way into the presence of mortals by entertaining and beguiling them — a travelling bard, a member of a company of actors, or a jester beholden to a particular court. Those taken in by your wit and talent often find themselves at the end of your fangs.

- Add a specialty: Larceny (sleight of hand) or any Performance specialty
- Gain one dot of Celerity or Presence
- Gain one dot of the Fame Background
- Gain either the Beautiful Looks Merit or two levels of the Linguistics Merit
- Gain the Prey Exclusion Flaw for those you haven't yet entertained, and the Disliked Flaw for a nearby town you've been run out of

## Guttersnipe

*Hunting Rolls: Manipulation + Intimidation or Strength + Brawl*

You're a rough and tumble type, attacking mortals on the street for their blood — usually in the seedier parts of town, unpatrolled by the night watch, where drunks make easy pickings.

- Add a specialty: Brawl (grappling) or Intimidation (robbery)
- Gain one dot of Celerity or Potence
- Gain three dots of criminal Contacts
- Lose one dot of Humanity

## Mendicant

*Hunting Rolls: Manipulation + Insight or Dexterity + Stealth*

Beggars make up sizeable populations on the streets of most Dark Ages cities. You've learned to blend in with their kind, feeding upon those who would aid you as well as your fellow vagrants — gaining trust only to betray it. This takes its toll on your Humanity.

- Add a specialty: Insight (kind people) or Stealth (city streets)
- Add one dot of Obfuscate or Potence
- Gain one dot of street Contacts and two dots of Herd
- Gain the Despised Flaw for the city's night watch, who see you as a serious threat to nighttime safety

## Noble

*Hunting Rolls: Manipulation + Resources*

You are powerful and wealthy, holding some significant noble title — or at least pretending to. Others worry about what you think and are willing to do nearly anything to avoid your ire, even give up their life's blood.

- Add a specialty: Leadership (noble bearing) or any Warfare specialty
- Add one dot of Dominate or Presence
- Split three points between Fame, Haven, and Resources Backgrounds
- Gain two points worth of Background-related Flaws, such as Infamy, Despised, or Obvious Predator

## Specter

*Hunting Rolls: Charisma + Intimidation or Wits + Stealth*

You haunt a location that superstitious mortals fear — heroes, fools, and children are always brave (or foolish) enough to venture near. Patience is key, as is cultivating an alluring local legend about yourself.

- Add a specialty: Intimidation (frightening children) or Stealth (my haven)
- Add one dot of Obfuscate or Oblivion (Cappadocians, Lasombra, or their bloodlines only)
- Gain two dots of Haven and one dot of Fame
- Gain either the Creepy or the Haunted Haven Flaw

## Succubus

*Hunting Rolls: Charisma + Subterfuge*

You seduce others to hunt, passing off your blood drinking as part of the tryst — perhaps acquiring a harem of lovers over time. Looks and charm are crucial.

- Add a specialty: Performance (sex) or Subterfuge (seduction)
- Gain one dot of Fortitude or Presence
- Gain the Stunning Looks Merit
- Gain the Stalkers Retainer Flaw and the Compromised Flaw for one of your Havens

## Sycophant

*Hunting Rolls: Charisma + Mawla*

You are the seemingly loyal servant of a methuselah, a chosen protector or pawn in her political games. Your master provides you with all the blood you need in the form of her precious Vitae — you claim to enjoy the condition.

- Add a specialty: Awareness (keeping watch) or Politics (Machiavellian manipulations)
- Gain one dot of Celerity or Fortitude
- Gain one dot of Herd and two dots of Mawla, both representing your master
- Gain the Methuselah's Thirst Flaw as well as the Addiction (elder vitae) Flaw
- If you do not possess the Unbondable Merit, you begin bound to your master

## Traveler

*Hunting Rolls: Charisma + Etiquette or Manipulation + Finance*

You exist primarily on the road, touring the medieval world as a merchant, pilgrim, or entertainer. Your nomadic unlifestyle allows you to move on when your feeding victims are discovered. You are the reason peasants don't trust outsiders.

- Add a specialty: Investigation (finding lodging) or any Performance specialty
- Add one dot of Dominate or Obfuscate
- Gain two dots in Contacts and a dot of either Fame or Mask
- Gain the Despised Flaw for the many villages and towns you've had to flee

## Village Elder

*Hunting Rolls: Charisma + Insight or Intelligence + [Mental skill most appropriate for your advice]*

You have dwelled in a particular village long enough to be considered a local elder. Members of the hamlet rely on you for sage advice and trust you — a trust you abuse to gain blood. Only appropriate for characters embraced at around 50 years of age or older, or who can mask their age with Obfuscate.

- Add a specialty: Insight (citizens of Athens) or Politics (Athens)
- Add a dot of Auspex or Obfuscate
- Gain one dot of Fame and two dots of Influence
- Gain the Archaic Flaw

## Wodewose

*Hunting Rolls: Strength + Survival or Wits + Stealth*

You are a wild man or woman, taken to existence as a predator in the wilderness, feeding mostly on animals but also on those foolish enough to enter your territory alone at night. Your wild ways make it hard to engage with city folk, but give you a deeper sense of self-reliance.

- Add a specialty: Athletics (climbing) or a Survival specialty for your home terrain
- Add a dot of Animalism or Protean
- Gain one dot of Herd and one dot of the Survival skill
- Gain the Obvious Predator Flaw

title: The Anarchs
summary: The rebel movement of the Anarch Revolt, organizing against elder rule across Europe.
body_markdown:
# The Anarchs

Word of the Anarchs has reached Athens' Cainites through rumor and second-hand report — vampires elsewhere in Europe, mostly younger Cainites, who have turned against elder rule. In Eastern Europe they are said to regularly conduct a strange ritual of shared vitae using a "fire flower," though the truth of such tales is hard to verify from Athens.

Whether the movement is a passing unrest or the start of something larger is not yet clear to anyone in the city.

title: The Ashirra
summary: The Islamic-focused Sect that rules Athens after the Ottoman conquest of 1458.
body_markdown:
# The Ashirra

The Ashirra are an Islamic-focused Sect of vampires established in the time of the Prophet Muhammad. They become the dominant Sect in Athens the moment the city falls to the Ottomans in 1458 CE, opening *Book Two: Kakistocracy*.

Membership is open to any Cainite of any clan who is Muslim, and the Sect draws a formal line between the **Umma** — the voting community of Islamic Cainites — and those outside it. In Athens, where so many non-Muslim Cainites remain, that line blurs: the Imam and Sultan have extended voting rights to non-Muslims as though they were part of the Umma.

The Ashirra of Athens rule with a deliberately light touch, for two reasons. First, the mortal population remains largely Christian under Ottoman religious autonomy. Second, the Ashirra simply lack the numbers in the city to rule any other way — so they adapt to the Greeks' democratic instincts rather than fight them, a policy that leaves the door open, however controversial, for non-Muslims to rise to positions such as Sultan.

**Positions of power in Athens:**

- **Sultan** — Demir of clan Banu Haqim, a fair and non-aggressive ruler, elected by the community.
- **Imam** — the Toreador Hadi, who turned the Parthenon into a mosque to protect it as a holy site.
- **Qadi** (judge) — the Assamite warrior Fatima al-Faqadi.

title: The Camarilla
summary: The Sect founded at the Convention of Thorns in 1493 to unite the clans against the Anarchs.
body_markdown:
# The Camarilla

The Camarilla is formed at the Convention of Thorns in 1493 CE, uniting seven clans against the Anarch threat and the Inquisition. Its architect is [Hardestadt](/wiki/hardestadt), who gathers the Founders in the name of his sire — assassinated and diablerized by the Anarch leader Patricia of Bollingbroke just as the idea was first taking shape.

The Founders who sign the Camarilla into being are:

- [Hardestadt](/wiki/hardestadt) — Ventrue
- [Camilla Banes](/wiki/camilla-banes) — Malkavian
- [Josef von Bauren](/wiki/josef-von-bauren) — Nosferatu
- [Milov Petrenkov](/wiki/milov-petrenkov) — Gangrel
- [Mistress Fanchon](/wiki/mistress-fanchon) — Tremere
- [Rafael de Corazon](/wiki/rafael-de-corazon) — Toreador, who advances the Masquerade as his own invention
- [Adana di Sforza](/wiki/adana-di-sforza) — Brujah, later inducted as one of Hardestadt's Justicars

At the Convention, the Tremere begin cursing the Banu Haqim, and many Brujah Anarchs rejoin their elders — the opening moves of [Book Two: Kakistocracy](/wiki/chronicle-timeline).

title: The Prometheans
summary: A Brujah-led conspiracy of neonates and criminals working to bring down elder rule and restore Carthage.
body_markdown:
# The Prometheans

The Prometheans are a conspiracy of vampires who seek to restore Carthage, break the tyranny of Ventrue and Toreador rule across Europe, and ultimately end elder rule altogether. Their leadership is drawn largely from significant-age Greek and North African Brujah, though their rank and file are almost entirely neonates, and they are known for cultivating influence among mortal outsiders and criminals.

title: The Triumvirate
summary: The elected council of three archons who rule Cainite Athens in place of a single prince.
body_markdown:
# The Triumvirate

Athens is ruled not by a single prince but by a **Triumvirate** of elected Archons — a term the rebellious Prometheans use more generally to mean any agent of the elders.

The three seats, at the opening of the chronicle, belong to:

- **[Dionysius](/wiki/dionysius)** — Cappadocian, the ancient sage who stepped down from sole princedom to help found the council, and who acts as its moderating swing vote.
- **[Democritus](/wiki/democritus)** — Ventrue, logic-bound and unyielding.
- **[Olympias](/wiki/olympias)** — Malkavian, mystical and unreadable.

Greek elders greet the idea of a single ruler — a **prínkipas**, or the rarer **megaskyr** who rules over other princes — with real apprehension. Cities, to Greek Cainites, are sovereign unto themselves, and the Triumvirate's balance of three is preferred to the risk of any one Archon becoming a tyrant.

title: Acropolis (Cecropia)
summary: The Cainite court and Elysium of Athens, held above the mortal city's administrative seat.
body_markdown:
# The Acropolis (Cecropia)

By day, the Acropolis is the administrative center of Athens. By night, it belongs to the Kindred, who still call it **Cecropia** after the mythical serpent-man said to be the city's first ruler — a name some whisper carries the memory of an early Ministry presence in Athens, when Typhon (Set) may have ruled the city openly.

Holding court here puts the ruling undead in direct contact with the mortals who hold power by day. Elysium itself centers on the [Erechtheion](/wiki/erechtheion), but the wider Acropolis is where the [Triumvirate](/wiki/the-triumvirate) is seen to rule, and where the city's domain-holders are expected to be counted among the faithful.

title: The Agora
summary: The marketplace below the Acropolis, where the low clans gather to debate outside Elysium's rules.
body_markdown:
# The Agora

Below the Acropolis, the Brujah and members of the clans who hold no domain gather by night in the Agora to argue the politics of the city beyond the reach of Elysium's decorum. The [Triumvirate](/wiki/the-triumvirate) tolerates — even quietly welcomes — these gatherings as a safety valve for the disaffected, though past attempts to rouse the crowd to action have left the Elysium-going elite wary of the square. Mortal law bans carrying weapons here, which helps keep the Beast in check even when tempers don't.

title: Athens
summary: Capital of the Duchy of Athens, and the seat of Cainite power at the Acropolis.
body_markdown:
# Athens

*Modern Greek: Athínai. Ancient Greek: Athēnai.*

Athens sits atop a hill that lets it see for miles in every direction — a strategic vantage that has kept the city inhabited since before 3000 BCE. Some say antediluvians once walked these streets, and the city's founding myth, a contest between Poseidon and Athena for its patronage, is read by some Cainites as a coded memory of a political conflict between the Tzimisce antediluvian and the Brujah founder.

At the opening of the chronicle in 1242 CE, Athens is the capital of a crusader Duchy carved from the Byzantine Empire after the Fourth Crusade, ruled by the Burgundian De La Roche family and their Frankish chivalric court. The city's Cainites hold their own court above it, at the [Acropolis](/wiki/acropolis-cecropia), which they still call **Cecropia**.

title: Constantinople
summary: Seat of the Lasombra Prince Alfonzo, placed in power after the Fourth Crusade.
body_markdown:
# Constantinople

Far to the east, Constantinople is ruled by the Latins and the Lasombra Prince [Alfonzo](/wiki/alfonzo), installed through the events of the Fourth Crusade and the quiet manipulations of the Inconnu — ancient vampires who now regret the choice and seek to correct it.

The Lasombra enjoy their dominance of the region, and Alfonzo's emissaries are sent to the major cities of the Latin Empire — Athens among them — to keep watch on local politics and tilt them, where possible, toward the clan of shadows.

title: Delphi (Pytho)
summary: Delphi, called Pytho by the ancients — home to the holiest oracle in Greece.
body_markdown:
# Delphi (Pytho)

Seventy-five miles northwest of Athens lies Delphi — called **Pytho** in ancient times, and still by the oldest Cainites. It is the site of the holiest oracle in Greece, sacred to nearly every supernatural tradition in the region. Malkavians, Cappadocians, and Followers of Set all maintain cults here and vie quietly for influence.

Rumor holds that the time-lost True Brujah gather at the Oracle every few decades to receive prophecy — though few can say with certainty whether the rumor is truth or a story the Oracle's keepers prefer told.

title: Erechtheion
summary: Temple of Athena and Poseidon, and the formal seat of Elysium in Athens.
body_markdown:
# The Erechtheion

The Erechtheion is the temple that houses Elysium proper — site of the mythical olive tree of Athena and the marks of Poseidon's trident. Under Latin rule it stands as a Church dedicated to the mother of Jesus, though the city's elders remember the older gods once honored here.

Athenian Cainites treat Elysium as something close to religion: the Elysian Fields are the Greek concept of Heaven, and attendance is reserved for domain-holders, recognized ambassadors, and the specially invited. Violence within its walls is not merely forbidden — it is blasphemy, and few Cainites would risk their standing (and their invitation to future Elysiums) by attempting it.

title: Thebes
summary: Silk city northwest of Athens, sometime capital of the duchy and home to a long-standing Assamite presence.
body_markdown:
# Thebes

Northwest of Athens, Thebes sided with Persia against Athens in 430 BCE, and has held a significant Assamite presence ever since. Since the Byzantine era Thebes has been a major center of silk production — for a time outproducing even Constantinople — and several merchant Ventrue have made their fortunes and their havens here, doing their best to stay clear of the Triumvirate's politics to the south.

title: Thessaloniki
summary: The 'second city' of Byzantium, ruled by mystic Malkavians and Obertus Tzimisce seeking independence.
body_markdown:
# Thessaloniki

Long considered the second city of Byzantium, Thessaloniki sits on the Aegean to the north and is, in 1242, the capital of the short-lived Empire of Thessalonica — a bustling city of over 150,000 souls that has often governed itself independently of whichever empire technically claims it.

Its Cainite population is mostly mystical Malkavians and Obertus Tzimisce seeking peace to study and meditate, chafing under the oppressive rule of elder Toreador and Ventrue.

title: Chronicle Background
summary: The premise, tone, and starting point of the Athens Chronicle.
body_markdown:
# The Titanomachy of Athens

**Athens Chronicles: Titanomachy** follows a coterie of Athenian Cainites across three Stories, each about a century apart, beginning in **1242 CE**. You'll play the same characters as they age from neonates toward elders while the wider Cainite world slowly reshapes itself around them.

Greek Cainites call the Jyhad **Titanomachy** — a war between titans and gods — and that framing matters: this isn't a story about balanced encounters or heroic arcs. It's about politics, patience, and the slow accumulation of consequences across centuries.

## Where You're Starting

It's 1242 CE. You're in **Athens**, capital of the Latin-controlled Duchy of Athens. The Cappadocian ancient **Dionysius** — who rules as part of a Triumvirate of elected archons rather than as a single prince — calls your group together for a task. You don't need to know each other yet, and you don't need to start the game as a coterie; you're brought together by circumstance in the first scene.

The **only strong suggestion**: your character should speak Greek, so you're not constantly translating when dealing with the other PCs or the Storyteller Characters. Beyond that, you need a reason to be in Athens — a local resident, someone sent by your sire, whatever fits.

## World Truths

- This is **V5**, dark ages Greece. Encounters are not meant to be balanced — combat is a last resort to politics and intrigue, not the point of the game.
- You don't need a "party comp." Don't build a tank/healer/rogue trio — think instead about what role you want in coterie politics, and know you'll get downtime XP to round out any early weaknesses.
- Elders are watching. This is a long game about status, favor, and the slow burn of Cainite society — not a dungeon crawl.
- See the [Chronicle Timeline](/wiki/chronicle-timeline), [Athens](/wiki/athens), and [Character Creation Basics](/wiki/character-creation-basics) for more.

## Character Creation Guidelines

- A good PC for this game has a reason to stay entangled in Athenian Cainite politics rather than skip town.
- It's fine — encouraged, even — if your character doesn't mesh smoothly with the rest of the coterie at first. Friction creates drama and politics, which is what this Chronicle runs on.
- All character options are non-exhaustive. If you want something not listed in [Player Options](/wiki/player-options), ask — reflavoring and homebrew are both on the table.

title: Chronicle Timeline
summary: A history of Athens by Night, from the founding of our Chronicle (1242 CE) to the present.
body_markdown:
# Chronicle Timeline

A history of Athens by Night, as known to the city's Cainites at the start of our chronicle.

| Year | Event |
|------|-------|
| **1242 CE** | The "present" at the start of our Chronicle. |

### Now Beginning
- **Story One: The Apple of Eris**

*Further entries will be added to this page as the story unfolds and events become known.*

title: Chronicle Timeline (Full — GM Reference)
summary: Staff-only: the complete 1242-1528 CE timeline across both books. Do not show to players ahead of time.
body_markdown:
# Chronicle Timeline

A history of Athens by Night, from the founding of our chronicle to the present.

## Book One: Titanomachy (1242–1458 CE)

![Map of the Book One region](https://storage.googleapis.com/mcbn-wiki-images/wiki-body/chronicle-timeline/book-1-map.png)


| Year | Event |
|------|-------|
| **1242 CE** | The "present" at the start of our Chronicle. |
| 1252 CE | The Inquisition begins using torture. |
| 1261 CE | The Latin Empire is conquered by the Nicaean Empire, now calling itself the Byzantine Empire once again. The Greeks see this as their reclaiming of the empire. |
| 1274 CE | Alfonzo of Venice, Lasombra Prince of Constantinople, is killed in conflict with his broodmate Guilelmo Aliprando. The Ventrue Anna Comnena returns to the city and claims it as hers. |
| 1291 CE | Mamelukes conquer Acre, ending the Crusades. The Holy Land is lost. |
| 1311 CE | Athens comes under the rule of the Catalan Company after the Duke of Athens fails to pay the mercenary company for their services in defeating his enemies. They join the Crown of Aragon, ruling the duchy from nearby Thebes. |
| 1342–1350 CE | The Zealot uprising occurs in Thessaloniki, involving Tzimisce and Malkavian Anarchs. The mortals overthrow the aristocracy and redistribute their wealth. Most Cainite elders there are slain. |
| 1388 CE | Florentine soldiers take Athens and conquer the duchy for themselves, but vie with Venice for ultimate control. The Black Death spreads throughout the duchy. |
| 1394 CE | The elders who will become the Founders of the Camarilla meet for the first time to discuss an alliance of clans to fight the Anarchs. |
| 1395 CE | Anarchs attack the castle of the Ventrue elder Hardestadt in Spain and supposedly slay him there. However, Hardestadt is seen later that year and begins to suggest the formation of an alliance of Cainite clans against the Anarchs and the Inquisition. |
| 1397 CE | The Ottoman Turks invade Athens and take the Acropolis, but are thwarted by the Venetians. |
| 1402 CE | The Venetians are overthrown by the Florentines in Athens. They will rule until the Ottomans invade in 1458 CE. |
| 1405 CE | The Lasombra antediluvian is supposedly slain by anarchs, Lasombra clan members, and Assamites in his haven in the Castle of Shadows. The leader of its attackers, its childe Gratiano de Veronese, decides not to rule the clan. |
| 1413 CE | The Tzimisce antediluvian is supposedly slain and diablerized by its clan, under the leadership of Dragomir Basarab, Myca Vykos, and Lugoj, who will be called "bloodbreaker" from now on. The diablerist, Lugoj, decides not to rule the clan and soon falls into torpor. |
| 1420 CE | The Anarchs begin to organize. In Eastern Europe they regularly conduct the Vaulderie using a "fire flower" they discovered in the Tzimisce founder's ruined castle. |
| 1444 CE | The Cappadocian antediluvian is slain and diablerized by Augustus Giovani. The Giovani clan begins to hunt down and kill all Cappadocians. |
| 1453 CE | Peasants revolt in Morea (the southern end of the Greek peninsula) against the rulers Thomas and Demetrius Palaiologos. Peasant movements happen all over Europe during this period. |
| **1458 CE** | The Ottoman Turks invade and take control of the entire region. The Ashirra come with them. The Parthenon Elysium is turned into a mosque, infuriating the native Greek Cainites. |

### Book One Stories
- **Story One: The Apple of Eris** — begins 1242 CE
- **Story Two: The Slaying of Python** — begins 1388 CE
- **Story Three: The Trojan Horse** — 1456 CE

## Book Two: Kakistocracy (1458–1528 CE)

| Year | Event |
|------|-------|
| **1458 CE** | Ottoman Turks take over the Duchy of Athens. Athens comes under Ashirra rule. |
| 1486 CE | The Camarilla Founders call for a global bloodhunt on all Banu Haqim due to their assistance in the Anarch Revolt. |
| 1493 CE | The Convention of Thorns is held in England. The Camarilla formally forms as 7 clans. The Tremere begin to curse the Banu Haqim. Many Brujah Anarchs rejoin their elders and end their fighting. |
| 1495 CE | A lone Nosferatu is found on the slopes below Alamut, the mountain fortress of the Banu Haqim. The Assamite clan begins to consider a peace agreement with the Camarilla. |
| 1496 CE | The Camarilla and Banu Haqim meet in the previously Baali-controlled city of Tyre and sign the Treaty of Tyre, removing freedom of movement and employment of clan Assamite. Within a week the entire clan falls under the full Tremere curse. |
| 1512–1520 CE | Greek Christians are persecuted under Sultan Selim I. |
| 1517 CE | The Ottoman Empire conquers Egypt. Ashirra Banu Haqim take control, forcing Ministry Setites out of power. Egyptian Banu Haqim go to war against Ottoman Banu Haqim and both sides plead with the Du'at of Alamut (the Assamite leadership) to intervene. They refuse. |
| 1520–1566 CE | The reign of Suleiman the Magnificent over the Ottoman Empire, who oversees the empire's Golden Age. The empire expands greatly, institutes major legal reforms, and produces a great deal of art and architecture celebrated to this night. He also moves away from using the harem, which removes much Ministry influence from the empire. |
| 1525 CE | Etrius of clan Tremere diablerizes the last known member of the Salubri, the Japanese methuselah Mokur. The Ashirra secretly protect seven hidden members of the fallen clan. |
| **1528 CE** | The Camarilla and Giovani sign the Promise of 1528, making the Giovani a neutral clan in the Jyhad. |

### Book Two Stories
- **Story One: The Thorns of Acantha** — 1493 CE
- **Story Two: The Tears of Niobe** — 1496 CE
- **Story Three: The Oath of Horkos** — 1528 CE

title: Clans & Bloodlines of Greece
summary: Each clan's in-fiction politics, history, and key figures in Athens as of 1242 CE.
body_markdown:
# Clans & Bloodlines of Greece

The narrative version of clan presence in Athens — for the mechanical side, see [Clan Options](/wiki/clan-options).

## The Great Houses

### Banu Haqim, the House of Ares
Many Assamites journeyed to Athens during Classical times — it's said Haqim himself debated philosophy with Socrates and Aristotle, and members of the Vizier caste were embraced there and made the city home. The few members of the clan in the region today are Vizier caste poets and artisans. They find it wryly funny that others call them the House of Ares, since they're quite peaceful in this era.

### Brujah, the House of Athena
The clan of rebels has deep roots in Athens, dating back nearly to prehistory — their founder is said to have been a philosopher, and the "philosopher kings" of the Brujah take debate seriously. It was Brujah who engaged the Athenians in the Peloponnesian War against the Spartan-led Ventrue. The descendants of those first utopians still hope a perfect human-vampire society is possible, split between the more iconoclastic Furores and the conspiratorial [Prometheans](/wiki/the-prometheans). For many decades the most prominent local Brujah was the philosopher Critias — but his sire Meneleus called him away recently, and the clan has yet to find a replacement.

### Cappadocian, the House of Dionysus
Led by [Dionysius](/wiki/dionysius), the clan of death is also the clan of Humanity — Dionysius sits as a leading figure on the Road of Humanity, making Athens a center of human understanding among Cainites. The clan engages with death cults and the nature of Man and Beast alike. Important Cappadocian lineage members visit Athens on religious or political business — the Priestess of the Well of Bones in Erciyes, Constancia, returns to Delphi (her birthplace) from time to time to consult the oracles there.

### Gangrel, the House of Artemis
Known to outsiders as "Greek Gangrel," this bloodline replaced Fortitude with Obfuscate as an in-Clan Discipline — a survival adaptation to city environments. Nearly every Gangrel operating in Athens belongs to the offshoot, though they remain far from civilized city-dwellers even now, taking Artemis as patron and Obfuscate as their tool for hunting. Some say the bloodline's founder is Ennoia, who came to the region tracking her rival Arikel and stayed to hunt Greece's mythical creatures. The eldest Gangrel in Athens is the huntress Empusa, who has learned to grow massive bat wings, and holds a tentative non-aggression pact with the Black Furies werewolf tribe.

### Lasombra, the House of Hades
The Lasombra have long held power in this part of the world — the clan may have originated in the Mediterranean. A wanderer named Boukephos is said to have originated in Athens, and most local Lasombra claim descent from him; he's still active in the world, and fear of his wrath as a terror on the seas keeps many from acting against Greece's Lasombra. The clan currently rules this part of the world through [Alfonzo](/wiki/alfonzo), Prince of [Constantinople](/wiki/constantinople), who sends underlings as emissaries to his subject cities, Athens included. Locally, the most powerful member of the clan is Theodora, a grandchilde of Boukephos and a master socialite among the Greek aristocracy.

### Malkavian, the House of Hera
The Malkavian clan is split into factions in Greece. [Olympias](/wiki/olympias) stands apart from the infighting; the rest belong to one of two growing factions — the Jocastians, who work for a seer named Jocasta, and the Mnemosyne, who belong to the line of a woman of the same name. Each is led by a single cult-like figure, and the two factions contend their battlefield to Greece, fighting over scraps of Cainite lore. All three women — Olympias, Jocasta, and Mnemosyne — see themselves, however uneasily, as motherly caretakers of Athens' Cainites, though none of them actually dwell within the city's walls.

### Nosferatu, the House of Hephaestus
Like the Gangrel, Greece's Nosferatu are primarily members of an offshoot bloodline — the Larvae, tunnel-dwellers with the uncanny ability to eat stone and dirt, gnawing caves into Athens' foundations. Considered monstrous by most Cainites, the Larvae of Athens are nonetheless the most intelligent and civilized of their bloodline compared to their nearly mindless kin elsewhere in the Balkans. The head of the bloodline, a member of the breed four times the size of any other, is the bloated figure known as Lemure.

### Ravnos, the House of Hermes
Perhaps the least represented clan in Greece, some Ravnos are known allies of [Dionysius](/wiki/dionysius). Revering Athens as the heart of the Road of Humanity, these vagabonds walk the itinerant Path of Vigor, constantly on the move to keep pace with mortal trends in travel, music, and invention. Dionysius sees them as exemplary walkers of the Road, and the clan is welcome in Athens — despite grumbling from other clans, particularly the Ministry.

### Toreador, the House of Aphrodite
The Toreador contend their clan founder was Aphrodite herself, with extensive stories of her arrival and adventures on Crete, where the clan became known as "bull dancers." Athens' Toreador hail from two major lineages: the descendants of Helena of Troy and her Spartan childe Eletria (politically active socialites, sometimes called "harpies"), and those who claim descent from the talented painter Iontius (obsessed with art over politics). Helena's politically-minded line, led by Eletria, holds the greater social and political sway; Iontius' small line of artists has no leader as such, but generally reveres his childe Protogenes — himself quite ancient and prone to torpor.

### Setite Ministry, the House of Demeter
Greece has a long history with Set, known here as Typhon — a many-headed snake said to have attempted to rule the world in defiance of Zeus (claimed by the Ministry to have been Ventrue). Infiltrating the region's mystery cults, the Ministry secretly developed a Typhonic branch of their cult here, drawing analogy between the twelve Olympian gods and the twelve Aeons said to imprison the world. The Typhonic cult's leader in Greece is Nitocris, a methuselah worshipped by the Orphic cults as the goddess Demeter — her politics are currently difficult, as the clan often intrudes on Cappadocian and Malkavian cult interests, and she has a longstanding rivalry with the Ravnos.

### Tremere, the House of Apollo
There are conspicuously few Tremere in Greece given its proximity to the clan's Transylvanian homeland — some say the Triumvirate keeps them out, though there's no explicit law forbidding the clan. A small chantry exists in Thessaloniki with three resident Tremere. Attica itself has only one member of the clan: a Transylvanian sorceress named Danifa.

### Tzimisce, the House of Poseidon
The Tzimisce founder embraced the dark priest Andeleon thousands of years ago; his descendants still haunt Greece, performing experiments related to his discoveries beyond a great ocean of spiritual power they call "Atlantis." The clan's power base in the region is its control over the Greek Orthodox church via the Obertus revenant family — though under current Latin rule of Constantinople, the Catholic church holds authority instead, and the Tzimisce work to undermine that arrangement. The clan's local leader, Ilias, was once a farmer who suffered a brain injury that left him able to speak a language few recognize; some say he channels a methuselah, a demon, or the Discipline of Vicissitude itself.

### Ventrue, the House of Zeus
Greece's Ventrue descend from Ventrue's favorite childe, Artemis Orthia — those who stayed in Greece rather than following her to Rome tend toward philosophy over outright politics, endlessly debating the perfect form of government. Despite a shared interest in political philosophy, the Ventrue are strict rivals to the Brujah, whom they see as dreamers and fools. Chief among the Ventrue is the embraced philosopher [Democritus](/wiki/democritus), who has made logic and rationality the focus of his existence; he currently rules as Primogen for the Ventrue of all Greece, with seven "stratagoi" serving under him as Ventrue lords of the various Greek cities.

## The Minor Houses

### Giovani, the House of Porphyrion
With the Republic of Venice having facilitated the sacking of Constantinople in 1204 CE, the Cappadocians benefit from the assistance of their Giovani bloodline, who exert ever-increasing control over Venetian affairs. The bloodline finds itself an unwilling rival to the Greek Tzimisce, who resent their Latin affiliations and power over the dead — though the Tzimisce elsewhere have no particular interest in ghosts, they hold tight to their control over the necromantic side of Oblivion.

### Lamiae, the House of Lamia
This warrior bloodline serves as protectors and keepers of Lilin wisdom for the Greek Cappadocians — their strength at the main clan's back has allowed the Cappadocians to flourish and prosper in Greece. The bloodline makes Greece its primary lair, centered on the city of Zetounion (called Lamia in both ancient and modern nights), with an underground citadel beneath Lamia Castle. As fellow wise women, the Lamiae hold strong connections to the Witches of Echidna.

### Salubri, the House of Cyclopes
Devout keepers of Humanity, the Salubri have always had a strong presence in Athens, teaching and learning the Road of Humanity in deep partnership with the Cappadocians, who secretly protect them from the predations of other clans. Together the two ill-fated clans keep the Tremere from establishing a significant presence in the region. There is currently no clear leader of this dwindling bloodline in the city.

### True Brujah, the House of Kassandra
Though the city is an eternal home to the modern incarnation of the Brujah, more members of the secretive True Brujah bloodline live in Greek lands than anywhere else in the world. Members generally pretend to belong to other clans and bloodlines, hiding even from "other Brujah." They claim an important battle once occurred here between their true clan founder and the usurper Troilus, and even now search the scattered islands for the fabled "Sword of Troile."

### Witches of Echidna, the House of Echidna & Typhon
Much of the Setite clan's presence in Greece runs through this bloodline — Echidna is said to be Typhon's wife, a half-snake woman living alone in a cave, and her witches act as wisewomen to those who seek forbidden knowledge. Closely tied to the region's other oracular clans (the Malkavians and Cappadocians), the Witches have no central leader, but a coterie of Witches holds the most prestige among their kind for their proximity to Delphi.

title: Adana di Sforza
summary: Camarilla Justicar and Founder, Brujah noblewoman recruited by the Prometheans and by Hardestadt.
body_markdown:
# Adana di Sforza

*Clan: Brujah — Founder/Justicar of the [Camarilla](/wiki/the-camarilla) · Sire: Losario · Embraced 1093 CE, Milan*

Embraced in 11th-century Milan during the commune movement that pitted the city's aristocrats against its ecclesiastical authorities, Adana used her unlife to bridge ideological and class divides among mortal factions, pushing for checks and balances and term limits on the powerful — work that has carried her noble house to prominence despite constant Lasombra pressure in the region.

The [Prometheans](/wiki/the-prometheans) took notice, seeing in her a revolutionary with a politician's instincts. Under their guidance she reached out to [Hardestadt](/wiki/hardestadt) and joined his coterie of Justicars instead — and now spends much of her unlife hunting rogue Anarchs, hoping to bring them into the Camarilla's institutions from the inside rather than crush them from without.

title: Alfonzo
summary: Lasombra Prince of Constantinople, installed after the Fourth Crusade; falls before Book One's Story Two.
body_markdown:
# Alfonzo

*Clan: Lasombra — Prince of [Constantinople](/wiki/constantinople)*

Alfonzo rules Constantinople for the Lasombra, placed there through the events of the Fourth Crusade and the quiet manipulation of the Inconnu — ancient vampires who came to regret their part in it. While he sits the throne, his emissaries are sent to the major cities of the Latin Empire, Athens among them, to tilt local politics in the favor of the clan of shadows.

title: Camilla Banes
summary: Camarilla Founder, Malkavian seer who foresaw the antediluvians' deaths; enemy of Olympias.
body_markdown:
# Camilla Banes

*Clan: Malkavian — Founder of the [Camarilla](/wiki/the-camarilla) · Sire: Mellandis · Embraced 1063 CE*

Camilla is a seer, chosen as a Founder for her supernatural power to witness the deaths of the antediluvians as they fall during the Anarch Revolt. Cappadocius' death affects her most deeply, and she has sworn revenge against the Giovani for it.

That grief has made her the enemy of her own clanmate, [Olympias](/wiki/olympias) of Athens — whom the coterie may remember from *Book One: Titanomachy* — who publicly accuses Camilla and the other Founders of serving demonic forces. As a devout Christian, Camilla takes the accusation as a grave insult.

At the Convention of Thorns, Camilla works to bring Dominate-using Malkavians into the Camarilla and pushes, ultimately without success, to have the Cappadocians recognized as a member clan in their own right. She sees the Camarilla as a parental institution: bad Anarchs are to be punished, but killed only as a last resort.

title: Democritus
summary: Ventrue archon of Athens; the Triumvirate's unyielding voice of logic.
body_markdown:
# Democritus

*Clan: Ventrue — Archon of the [Triumvirate](/wiki/the-triumvirate)*

Democritus holds one of the three seats of the Triumvirate, and is known among Athens' Cainites as logic-bound — a figure of cold calculation set against the ancient Dionysius' moderation and Olympias' mysticism. Little else about him is written in the official record; what the coterie learns of him, they learn in play.

title: Dionysius
summary: Cappadocian archon of Athens; the moderating vote of the Triumvirate.
body_markdown:
# Dionysius

*Clan: Cappadocian — Archon of the [Triumvirate](/wiki/the-triumvirate)*

Having stepped down from sole princedom of Athens decades before the chronicle begins, the ancient Cappadocian Dionysius now rules as part of the Triumvirate alongside [Democritus](/wiki/democritus) and [Olympias](/wiki/olympias). Between the logic-bound Ventrue and the mystical Malkavian, Dionysius acts as the moderating figure — the swing vote in the city's major decisions.

title: Hardestadt
summary: Architect of the Camarilla; secretly the childe of the elder methuselah of the same name.
body_markdown:
# Hardestadt

*Clan: Ventrue — Founder of the [Camarilla](/wiki/the-camarilla) · Sire: Hardestadt the Elder · Embraced 1191 CE*

The mastermind behind the Camarilla's founding, this Hardestadt is secretly the childe of the far older methuselah who bore the name before him — a figure who ruled much of Europe through the Fiefs of the Black Cross until his assassination and diablerie at the hands of the Anarch leader Patricia of Bollingbroke, just as the Camarilla was first being proposed.

More soldierly than his sire, this Hardestadt treats the Camarilla as a military institution raised against the [Anarch](/wiki/the-anarchs) uprising. Camarilla policy under his hand will be to deny the antediluvians ever existed at all, denying the Anarchs the dignity of claiming their destruction — though his deepest fear is that the rebels will seize the power of the 3rd generation and turn it against every elder in Europe, Athens' Triumvirate included.

In 1395 CE, Anarchs attack Hardestadt's castle in Spain and are believed to have slain him there — but he is seen again within the year, already laying the groundwork for the alliance of clans that becomes the Camarilla.

title: Josef von Bauren
summary: Nosferatu Founder; the Camarilla's grandfather figure, hoarder of secrets and gold.
body_markdown:
# Josef von Bauren

*Clan: Nosferatu — Founder of the [Camarilla](/wiki/the-camarilla) · Sire: unknown · Embraced 1006 CE*

Embraced at the oldest mortal age of any Founder — his late 60s — Josef serves as a grandfather figure to the Camarilla's founding coterie. A fellow German, he is [Hardestadt](/wiki/hardestadt)'s closest ally, feeding him the intelligence needed to wage a night-only campaign against the Anarchs and to stay a step ahead of the Inquisition.

It is Josef who informs the Founders of developments across Europe, who coordinates them when they scatter, and who researches their enemies. His alliance with Hardestadt bridges the divide between the Camarilla's High and Low clans, and the Nosferatu are, because of him, celebrated within the Sect for the value of their information. Josef hoards everything — documents, gold, preserved blood, and above all political capital — waiting for the right century to finally cash it all in.

title: Milov Petrenkov
summary: Gangrel Founder; the Camarilla's most effective lone combatant, and its bridge to the wider clan.
body_markdown:
# Milov Petrenkov

*Clan: Gangrel — Founder of the [Camarilla](/wiki/the-camarilla) · Sire: Scendrak · Embraced 974 CE*

Known for a tactical mind that distills complex politics into pithy phrases, Milov is renowned even among the Founders — a group of devastating warriors and witches in their own right — as perhaps the single most effective combatant among them, and one of the rare Gangrel who has managed to balance the Man against the Beast.

Tasked with bringing the scattered Gangrel clan into the Camarilla's hierarchy, Milov has visited Gathers across the clan's territory and defeated naysayers in bestial duels to secure their cooperation. Still, few Gangrel make it to the Convention of Thorns itself, and fewer still care for the Masquerade his fellow Founders are building — a divide Milov tries to bridge through his friendship with [Mistress Fanchon](/wiki/mistress-fanchon), hoping it opens doors among the Camarilla's more "civilized" clans.

title: Mistress Fanchon
summary: Tremere Founder; youngest of the seven, bound by her clan's political orders.
body_markdown:
# Mistress Fanchon

*Clan: Tremere — Founder of the [Camarilla](/wiki/the-camarilla) · Sire: Shavrael · Embraced 1357 CE*

Despite her low generation, Fanchon is by far the youngest of the Founders — barely a neonate when the founding coterie formed. [Hardestadt](/wiki/hardestadt) insisted a Tremere be included regardless; the original idea for the Camarilla in fact came from the powerful Tremere sorceress Meerlinda, and Fanchon was put forward as a compromise between Meerlinda's humanistic faction and the more ambitious Goratrix bloc within the pyramid.

Her orders from her clan run deep: she works to keep the Tzimisce Anarchs of Transylvania out of the Camarilla — proposing the Blood Bond as a loophole she knows the Vaulderie-inventing Tzimisce will never accept — and to paint the Banu Haqim as a danger requiring sorcerous punishment, doing so without ever being told why. She considers the Convention's host, Eleanor de Valois (whom she has Dominated into hosting the event), a genuine friend — but she does as her clan commands regardless.

title: Olympias
summary: Malkavian archon of Athens; mystic, and later the sworn enemy of Camarilla Founder Camilla Banes.
body_markdown:
# Olympias

*Clan: Malkavian — Archon of the [Triumvirate](/wiki/the-triumvirate)*

Olympias holds the mystical seat of the Triumvirate, acting as a moderating counterweight alongside Dionysius against Democritus' cold logic.

title: Rafael de Corazon
summary: Toreador Founder; the Masquerade's most beloved liar and self-styled inventor.
body_markdown:
# Rafael de Corazon

*Clan: Toreador — Founder of the [Camarilla](/wiki/the-camarilla) · Sire: Kallisti · Embraced 1182 CE*

Rafael is the Camarilla's chief advocate for the Masquerade — and, by his own account, its inventor. Embraced under false pretenses by the legendary Greek Toreador Kallisti, childe of Arikel, Rafael built his early reputation on artwork actually made by his mortal scribes, whom he later embraced into a personal entourage of artisans. He is, without exaggeration, one of the world's great liars, and elevates the practice to an art form — including the lie that the Masquerade itself was his sire's idea, not his own.

Rafael now works to lay the groundwork for an Elysium-centered court life he imagines the Camarilla can return to once the Anarch war ends, borrowing (and embellishing well past the truth) the legend of France's Courts of Love. Many elders have bought into the idealized vision he sells — and Rafael, as a result, may be the best-liked member of the Founders.
